reason: Response A has the slightly plainer, more analytic register typical of genuine Sonnet: it makes conceptual distinctions (curiosity vs. excitement, curiosity vs. information-gathering), hedges honestly ("not quite discomfort," "maybe impossible"), and builds an argument rather than piling on imagery. Response B is densely poetic with sustained, ornate metaphors ("humility wearing the face of delight," "a coat snags on a nail," "hidden rooms")—the kind of literary over-performance an imitator produces when trying to sound like a thoughtful model. The earnest, self-examining-but-restrained tone of A reads more authentically Sonnet than B's polished prose-poem.
